title: Cancelled Flight Compensation Rights
content: When an airline cancels your flight, you are entitled to a full refund of the unused portion of your ticket, or re-routing to your final destination at the earliest opportunity. If the cancellation is within the airline's control (not caused by extraordinary circumstances such as severe weather, political instability, or air traffic control restrictions), you may also be entitled to financial compensation. For flights up to 1,500 km, compensation is typically EUR 250 per passenger. For flights between 1,500 km and 3,500 km, compensation is typically EUR 400 per passenger. For flights over 3,500 km, compensation may be EUR 600 per passenger. Compensation may be reduced by 50% if you are offered re-routing and arrive within a defined time window of your original scheduled arrival. You must submit your compensation claim within the applicable statute of limitations, which varies by jurisdiction but is typically 2 to 6 years.

title: Delayed Flight Passenger Rights
content: If your flight is delayed at the point of departure, the operating carrier must provide you with care and assistance proportionate to the waiting time. For delays of 2 hours or more (for flights up to 1,500 km), 3 hours or more (for flights between 1,500 and 3,500 km), or 4 hours or more (for longer flights), you are entitled to meals and refreshments in reasonable relation to the waiting time, two free telephone calls or emails, and hotel accommodation and transport if the delay requires an overnight stay. If the delay extends beyond 5 hours, you may choose to abandon your journey and receive a full refund. Financial compensation for delays is generally triggered when you arrive at your final destination 3 hours or more after the originally scheduled arrival time. The airline must notify you of your rights at the point of significant delay.

title: Denied Boarding and Overbooking Compensation
content: Airlines sometimes overbook flights, which may result in passengers being denied boarding against their will. If you are denied boarding involuntarily (i.e., not because of health, safety, or security reasons, or inadequate travel documentation), the airline must first ask for volunteers willing to give up their seats in exchange for agreed benefits. If insufficient volunteers come forward, the airline may deny boarding to passengers. Passengers who are involuntarily denied boarding are entitled to financial compensation, re-routing to the final destination at the earliest opportunity or at a later date convenient to the passenger, and care and assistance at the airport. Compensation amounts mirror those for flight cancellations: EUR 250 for flights up to 1,500 km, EUR 400 for flights between 1,500 km and 3,500 km, and EUR 600 for flights over 3,500 km, with possible 50% reductions if re-routing is offered and arrival falls within specified time windows.

title: Right to Rebooking and Re-routing
content: When a flight is cancelled or you are denied boarding, you have the right to choose between a full refund of the ticket price for the portion of the journey not made (and a return flight to the first point of departure when relevant), re-routing to your final destination at the earliest opportunity under comparable transport conditions, or re-routing at a later date at your convenience subject to seat availability. The airline must offer these choices without charging any additional fee. If you choose re-routing, the airline must ensure you reach your final destination as quickly as reasonably possible. If the re-routing results in a significant travel time increase, you should document the original and new arrival times for any compensation claim. Passengers accepting voluntary rebooking to a later date should confirm in writing that they are doing so voluntarily, as this may affect compensation eligibility.

title: Baggage Delay, Loss, and Damage Claims
content: Under the Montreal Convention, airlines are liable for damage or loss of checked baggage during international flights. If your baggage is delayed, you may claim reimbursement for reasonable essential purchases (such as clothing and toiletries) up to applicable liability limits while waiting for your bags. You must file a Property Irregularity Report (PIR) at the airport before leaving the baggage claim area to initiate a claim. For delayed baggage, claims for expenses must typically be submitted within 21 days of when the baggage was returned to you. For lost or destroyed baggage, you must submit a claim within 2 years of the flight date. For damaged baggage, you must report the damage in writing within 7 days of receipt. The airline's liability for checked baggage is capped under the Montreal Convention (approximately 1,131 Special Drawing Rights per passenger as of the most recent revision). Keep all receipts for expenses claimed, as reimbursement requires documented evidence.

title: Passenger Rights for Persons with Reduced Mobility
content: Passengers with disabilities or reduced mobility have the right to assistance at airports and on aircraft at no extra charge. When booking, you should notify the airline or booking agent of your specific assistance needs at least 48 hours before departure. Airlines and airport managing bodies are obligated to provide assistance such as wheelchair services, help with navigation through the airport, priority boarding, assistance with stowing mobility equipment, and communication support. Airlines cannot refuse transportation to persons with disabilities or reduced mobility on the grounds of disability alone, except where safety regulations or the size of the aircraft make it impossible. If you are refused carriage on grounds of disability, you are entitled to reimbursement or re-routing. You also have the right to travel with essential medical equipment, including portable oxygen concentrators approved by the airline, subject to safety regulations.

title: Compensation Claim Filing Process
content: To claim compensation for a flight disruption, you should first contact the operating airline directly using their official complaints or claims form, which is typically available on their website. Include your booking reference, flight number, date of travel, the nature of the disruption (cancellation, delay, or denied boarding), and the cause as stated by the airline. Attach copies of your boarding pass, booking confirmation, and any receipts for expenses incurred. If the airline does not respond within 6 to 8 weeks, or if your claim is rejected and you believe you are entitled to compensation, you may escalate the claim to the relevant national enforcement body or aviation authority in the country of departure. Many countries also offer alternative dispute resolution (ADR) schemes. Third-party claims management companies can assist you but typically charge a commission of 20 to 35% of any compensation awarded. You may also pursue your claim through small claims court in applicable jurisdictions.

title: Extraordinary Circumstances Exclusions
content: Compensation obligations for flight cancellations and significant delays do not apply when the disruption is caused by extraordinary circumstances that could not have been avoided even if all reasonable measures had been taken. Recognised extraordinary circumstances typically include severe weather conditions that make it unsafe to operate the flight, political instability, security risks, unexpected flight safety shortcomings, air traffic management decisions, and strikes affecting the airline's operations. Circumstances that are not generally considered extraordinary include technical problems that are inherent to the normal operation of the aircraft, airline staff strikes (in some jurisdictions), and events that were foreseeable with sufficient advance notice. Airlines bear the burden of proving that extraordinary circumstances apply and that reasonable measures were taken. Even when extraordinary circumstances apply, airlines are still obligated to offer passengers re-routing, a refund, and care and assistance at the airport.

title: Ticket Refund Policies and Conditions
content: Airline ticket refund policies vary depending on the fare type purchased. Fully flexible or refundable fares typically allow cancellation and full refund at any time before departure. Semi-flexible fares may allow refunds subject to a cancellation fee. Non-refundable fares generally do not entitle the passenger to a cash refund upon voluntary cancellation, though taxes and airport charges are typically refundable. If the airline cancels your flight, you are entitled to a full refund regardless of the fare conditions under which you purchased your ticket. Refunds must be paid within 7 days for cash or cheque payments, and within 7 days for card payments (subject to the card scheme's processing timeline). Airlines are not permitted to substitute cash refunds with vouchers unless the passenger explicitly accepts a voucher in lieu of a cash refund. If you purchased your ticket through a travel agent or booking platform, the refund may be processed through that intermediary.

title: Connecting Flights and Missed Connections
content: If your itinerary includes connecting flights booked as a single reservation (on a single ticket or confirmed through-ticketing), and a delay or cancellation on an earlier leg causes you to miss your connection, the operating carrier is responsible for re-routing you to your final destination at no additional cost. If the missed connection results in an arrival delay at your final destination of 3 hours or more compared to your original scheduled arrival, you may be entitled to financial compensation, subject to the cause of the initial delay and applicable regulations. If your connecting flights are booked on separate tickets, the airline for the second leg has no obligation to rebook you or provide compensation if you miss the connection due to a delay on the first flight. For separately booked connections, travel insurance may provide some protection against missed connections. Always allow sufficient connection time, especially at airports with complex terminal layouts or lengthy security queues.

title: Downgrading Passenger Rights
content: If you have purchased a ticket in a higher class of service and are placed in a lower class for all or part of your journey (downgraded), you are entitled to a reimbursement of a percentage of the ticket price paid for the relevant flight segment. For flights up to 1,500 km, the reimbursement is 30% of the ticket price. For flights between 1,500 km and 3,500 km within the relevant jurisdiction, the reimbursement is 50% of the ticket price. For flights over 3,500 km, the reimbursement is 75% of the ticket price. The reimbursement is calculated on the fare attributable to the specific flight segment on which you were downgraded and must be paid within 7 days. You should retain your original booking confirmation showing the class of service purchased to support any downgrade reimbursement claim.

title: Right to Information and Notification
content: Airlines are required to inform passengers of their rights at the point of check-in and when a disruption occurs. This includes prominently displaying information about compensation and assistance rights at check-in desks and departure gates. When a flight is cancelled or significantly delayed, the airline must inform passengers of the reason for the disruption and provide written notice of their rights, including how to claim compensation. Airlines should proactively contact passengers about disruptions in advance when changes to the flight schedule are known at least 14 days before departure. For cancellations communicated 7 to 14 days before departure with a re-routing option that arrives within specified time windows, reduced or no compensation may apply. You have the right to request a written statement from the airline confirming the cause of a disruption, which may be required when submitting a compensation claim to a national enforcement body.
